sound_type: chirping trill alarm -- rapid series of short, sharp chirps in quick succession, stuttering machine-gun quality, colony alarm trigger, E=4, first true grassland species
ecosystem: open grassland, sagebrush steppe, bunchgrass prairie, agricultural fields. Low elevation -- 200-1,500 meters. The OPPOSITE of alpine. First true grassland species in the engine.
season: active late winter through early summer (February-July); ESTIVATES in late summer heat and HIBERNATES through winter. Combined dormancy 7-8 months. Active only 4-5 months per year.

species_pair.pair_type: Sciuridae QUARTET -- FOURTH consecutive Sciuridae (75-76-77-78). Matches Mustelidae quartet (63-66) for longest family run. TIED. Three marmots (Marmota, 75-77) + one ground squirrel (Urocitellus, 78). Four acoustic signatures: chirp, whistle, multi-note whistle, chirping trill. Alpine to grassland habitat transition.

coordinates.description: Reference for open grassland habitat in the Columbia Basin, eastern Washington. Sagebrush steppe and bunchgrass prairie. The Townsend's Ground Squirrel inhabits the dry interior grasslands of the PNW.

taxonomy_note.description: FOURTH CONSECUTIVE SCIURIDAE (75-76-77-78). Sciuridae quartet. Ties Mustelidae (63-66). NEW GENUS Urocitellus -- ground squirrels, not marmots. Seventeenth mammalian entry. Fifteenth mammalian species. Fifth Rodentia. Sciuridae's fourth entry. Named for John Kirk Townsend (1834).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Urocitellus from Greek oura ('tail') + Latin citellus ('ground squirrel'). townsendii honors John Kirk Townsend (1809-1851), Philadelphia naturalist, 1834 Columbia River expedition. Described by Bachman (1839).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Urocitellus -- NEW GENUS in the engine. Ground squirrels (split from Spermophilus in 2009). Multiple species in western North America. The shift from Marmota (75-77) to Urocitellus (78) within the Sciuridae quartet.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- fifth Rodentia in the engine. Beaver (61-62), Yellow-bellied (75), Hoary (76), Olympic (77), Townsend's (78).
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- seventeenth mammalian entry. Fifteenth mammalian species. Four orders. Nine families. Two infraclasses.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Sciuridae's FOURTH entry at degree 78. Fourth consecutive (75-76-77-78). Sciuridae quartet ties Mustelidae quartet (63-66) for longest family run.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. ALARM TRILL. Rapid series of short chirps. Colony alarm trigger. Stuttering, machine-gun quality.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: 0.5-2.0 seconds per trill burst. Individual chirps: 0.05-0.1 seconds each.
song_metrics.note_structure: Rapid repetitive chirps -- many short identical notes in quick succession. No pitch variation between notes (unlike Olympic Marmot's multi-note call).
song_metrics.note_types: Alarm trill (primary, rapid chirping), contact call (softer chirps), distress call (high-pitched squeal).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Simple: rapid burst of identical chirps. The simplest structure in the Sciuridae quartet -- repetition rather than variation.
song_metrics.timbre: Sharp, dry, rapid. A staccato burst. Unlike the tonal whistles of the marmots, the ground squirrel's trill is percussive and rapid.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Moderate. Audible at 100-200 meters across flat grassland. Open habitat allows efficient sound propagation.
song_metrics.tempo: Very fast. Individual chirps at approximately 10-20 per second. The fastest vocal repetition rate in the Sciuridae quartet.
song_metrics.context: ALARM. Colony alarm trigger. One squirrel's trill sends entire colony diving for burrows. Distributed early warning network.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Alarm trill is consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: CHIRPING TRILL ALARM -- the 79th acoustic element. Rapid high-pitched chirping trill. E=4. Stuttering, machine-gun quality. Colony alarm trigger. First grassland acoustic element.

physical_description.overall: Small ground squirrel. 170-270 mm total length. 120-300 g. Gray-brown to sandy-brown above, lighter below. Cryptic coloring for grassland. Dramatically smaller than any marmot in the quartet.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 78: (1) SMALL SIZE -- 120-300 g. Much smaller than any marmot. (2) CRYPTIC GRAY-BROWN -- matches sagebrush steppe. (3) SHORT TAIL -- 35-70 mm. (4) OPEN GRASSLAND HABITAT -- not alpine. (5) COLONIAL -- dense burrow systems. (6) STANDS ERECT -- 'picket pin' posture.
physical_description.fur_detail: BODY: Gray-brown to sandy-brown above. Cryptic coloring matching sagebrush steppe. BELLY: Lighter, buff to white. FACE: Lighter gray, no dramatic markings. No dark 'boots' (contrast Hoary). No yellow belly (contrast Yellow-bellied). Plain and cryptic.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition. Herbivorous/granivorous. Grasses, seeds, forbs, some insects.
physical_description.field_id: In open grassland or sagebrush steppe: a small, gray-brown ground squirrel standing erect ('picket pin') or diving into a burrow. Much smaller than any marmot. Cryptic coloring. Colonial -- many individuals in suitable habitat. Rapid chirping trill alarm is diagnostic.

dormancy_note.description: DOUBLE DORMANCY: Estivation (late summer, when grassland dries) + Hibernation (autumn through winter). Combined dormancy 7-8 months. Active only 4-5 months per year (approximately February-July). The only species in the engine with both estivation and hibernation.
dormancy_note.contrast: Marmots (75-77) hibernate in winter but are active in summer. Townsend's Ground Squirrel estivates in summer AND hibernates in winter. Same family, opposite activity timing. The double dormancy is unique in the engine.
dormancy_note.significance: The double dormancy reflects the extreme seasonality of sagebrush steppe -- the brief window between cold winter and hot dry summer when vegetation is green.

nodes.title: Sciuridae Quartet (75-76-77-78) -- Ties Mustelidae Record
nodes.description: Four consecutive Sciuridae. Matches Mustelidae (63-66). Three marmots (Marmota, 75-77) + one ground squirrel (Urocitellus, 78). Four acoustic signatures. Alpine to grassland transition. Tied for longest family run.

nodes.title: Chirping Trill Alarm -- 79th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Rapid series of short, sharp chirps. 4,000-8,000 Hz. E=4. Stuttering, machine-gun quality. 10-20 chirps per second. Colony alarm trigger. First grassland acoustic element.

nodes.title: First True Grassland Species -- New Habitat
nodes.description: Open grassland, sagebrush steppe, bunchgrass prairie. 200-1,500 m. The engine descends from alpine (74-77) to grassland (78). First true grassland species. Sagebrush steppe is one of North America's most threatened ecosystems.

nodes.title: New Genus: Urocitellus
nodes.description: First ground squirrel genus in the engine. Shift from Marmota (75-77) to Urocitellus (78). Same family (Sciuridae), different genus, different body plan, different habitat.

nodes.title: Keystone Prey Species
nodes.description: Supports multiple predators: Red Fox (71), Short-tailed Weasel (67), Long-tailed Weasel (68), raptors, badgers, coyotes. The prey base of the grassland ecosystem. Colonial density makes it a reliable food source.

nodes.title: Double Dormancy -- Estivation + Hibernation
nodes.description: Estivates in late summer heat. Hibernates in winter. Combined dormancy 7-8 months. Active only 4-5 months (Feb-Jul). The only species in the engine with both dormancy types. Reflects extreme seasonality of sagebrush steppe.

nodes.title: Named for John Kirk Townsend (1834)
nodes.description: John Kirk Townsend, Philadelphia naturalist, 1834 Columbia River expedition with Thomas Nuttall. Collected foundational PNW specimens. Species described by Bachman (1839).

nodes.title: The Acid Tongue Parallel
nodes.description: Both communal (colonial burrows / band porch party). Both shift habitat (alpine to grassland / solo to band). Both at ground level (underground burrows / underground music). Three dimensions.

nodes.title: Red Fox Predator Connection (SPS-071)
nodes.description: Predator-prey. The fox hunts ground squirrels in grassland. Fox bark (71) vs. squirrel trill (78). Predator and prey across grassland.

nodes.title: Weasel Predator Connection (SPS-067)
nodes.description: Predator-prey. The Short-tailed Weasel enters squirrel burrows. Same dynamic as weasel-marmot and weasel-pika predation. The predator that goes underground.

nodes.title: Snowshoe Hare Prey Parallel (SPS-073)
nodes.description: Both colonial prey species supporting multiple predators. Hare in forest. Squirrel in grassland. Both hunted by fox and weasels. Same ecological role, different habitats.

cross_references.relationship: SCIURIDAE QUARTET. Marmot (77) to ground squirrel (78). Same family, different genus (Marmota to Urocitellus). Alpine endemic to open grassland. Multi-note whistle vs. chirping trill.
SCIURIDAE QUARTET (75-78). Three marmots + one ground squirrel. Four acoustic signatures. Alpine to grassland. Large (2-7 kg) to small (120-300 g).
PREY PARALLEL. Both colonial prey species supporting multiple predators. Forest (hare) vs. grassland (squirrel). Both hunted by fox and weasels. Same ecological role.
PREDATOR-PREY. Fox hunts ground squirrels in grassland. Fox bark (71) vs. squirrel trill (78). Predator and prey across open terrain.
PREDATOR-PREY. Weasel enters squirrel burrows. Same dynamic as weasel-marmot and weasel-pika predation. The predator that goes underground, repeated across the quartet.
Seventeenth mammalian entry. Fifteenth species. Fifth Rodentia. Sciuridae quartet (75-78) ties Mustelidae (63-66). New genus Urocitellus. First grassland species.
Open grassland/sagebrush steppe habitat. First grassland species. Colonial burrowing herbivore. Keystone prey. Double dormancy (estivation + hibernation). Sagebrush steppe threatened ecosystem.
